title: Vector Codebase Quality Audit — May 2026
topic: Full codebase deep-dive across 7 quality dimensions (Standards, Cleanliness, Approach, Readability, Manageability, Future-Proofing, Maintenance). Evidence gathered across 326 TS/TSX, 276 Go, 372 SQL, 70 docs, 47 PLA plans, and existing audit history.
summary: Mature, opinionated monolith. Backend layering excellent; frontend has size/inline-style hotspots; secrets posture is the single critical risk. Confidence: Medium-High.
content:
Contents
  1. Abstract
  2. 1. Methodology
  3. 2. Findings by Dimension
  4. 3. Cross-Cutting Observations
  5. 4. Case Studies
  6. 5. Recommendations
  7. 6. Conclusion
  8. Appendix

Abstract

Vector is an in-flight Next.js 15 + Go monolith (326 TS/TSX, 276 Go, 372 SQL migrations, 70 docs, 47 PLA plans) for a multi-tenant portfolio / work-item platform. The audit found a mature, opinionated codebase with unusually strong governance: 21 custom domain linters, paired up/down migrations, a documented "Trust No One" security model, a tech-debt register tied to every task, and a structured plan/scope/retro discipline. Backend Go is the clear strong point — clean handler/service/SQL layering, 100% context propagation, zero detected SQL-injection vectors, 50% test-to-code ratio. The frontend shows the wear of rapid iteration: 8 source files above 700 LoC (largest at 2,171 LoC), 92 inline style={{}} violations against its own CSS guide, 14 React Contexts, and a 1,029-LoC app/lib/apiSite/index.ts god-object. A parallel app/redesign/ shell coexists with the main shell. Five icon libraries are installed simultaneously. The single Critical finding is secrets posture (plaintext credentials in .env.local / .env.dev, three High-severity dangerouslySetInnerHTML sites — already catalogued in SA001.json). Test culture is selectively strong (50% backend ratio, dedicated featuretests and dbinvariants packages) but thin on the frontend (~6% ratio). Overall verdict: ship-grade once the secrets and XSS items in SA001 are closed; refactor pressure is concentrated in three identifiable hotspots rather than spread system-wide.

1. Methodology

The review combined four parallel evidence-gathering sweeps (frontend, backend, schema/docs/tooling, security/deps) with cross-referencing against the project's own artefacts: docs/c_security.md, docs/c_tech_debt.md, docs/c_c_backend_validation.md, docs/c_c_db_routing.md, MY_HANDOVER.md, the 47 dev/plans/PLA-*.json files, and the freshly-generated dev/security-audits/SA001.json. Static signals included wc -l for hotspot detection, grep sweeps for anti-patterns (style={{, dangerouslySetInnerHTML, fmt.Sprintf near SQL, context.Background(), interface{}, TODO/FIXME/HACK), package.json + go.mod dependency inspection, and migration-pairing checks across db/*/schema and db/*/schema/down.

Scope limitations: No runtime profiling, no live npm audit / govulncheck execution, no penetration testing. CSP and cookie-flag verification is partial (read from doc, not from running headers). The verdict on dynamic correctness (race conditions, leak detection, long-tail concurrency) is out of scope and would require runtime instrumentation.

2. Findings by Dimension

2.1 Coding Standards — Good

Evidence: 21 custom Python linters in package.json enforce domain-specific rules (writer boundary, SQL-in-sqlfile-only, role-literals, addressables, column-prefix convention) beyond what ESLint/golangci-lint cover. TypeScript strict-mode is observed: only 25 type-safety leaks across 326 frontend files (6 : any, 10 as unknown, 9 as any, 1 @ts-expect-error) — under 1%. Go interface{} usage is 11 occurrences and confined to JWT/JSON external contracts (backend/internal/auth/tokens.go, portfoliomodels/dev_reset.go).

Analysis: The custom linter set is unusual and load-bearing — it encodes architectural rules that would otherwise rot. It also explains the codebase's low TODO density (6 total across both backend and frontend): rules are enforced at commit time, so violations don't accumulate as comments.

2.2 Cleanliness — Fair

Evidence (hotspot files):

  • app/(user)/preferences/navigation/page.tsx — 2,171 LoC (DnD state + draft reconciliation + 50+ type defs inline)
  • app/components/ResourceTree.tsx — 1,706 LoC
  • app/(user)/workspace-admin/flow-states/page.tsx — 1,628 LoC (19 inline-style violations alone)
  • app/(user)/theme/page.tsx — 1,413 LoC
  • app/lib/apiSite/index.ts — 1,029 LoC (single registry for every backend route group)
  • backend/cmd/server/main.go — 1,652 LoC (boot wiring monolith)

Dead code is near-absent: only 6 TODO/FIXME total. Magic numbers appear in visualisation components (e.g. JourneyDomeChart.tsx L46-50: W=920, H=580, R_OUTER=360 with no constants module). Inline styles: 92 instances across app/, concentrated in flow-states, v2/compare, flow-rules, TimeboxManager, catalogue.

Analysis: The codebase is clean of cruft (no commented-out blocks, near-zero TODO debt) but accumulates size in three predictable shapes: admin pages with embedded state, generic primitive components (ResourceTree, ObjectTree), and central registries. The 92 inline-style violations are a known guide-breach (see docs/css-guide.md) that has not yet been ratcheted by lint.

2.3 Approach / Architecture — Good

Evidence — backend (Excellent): Handler/Service/SQL layering is strictly enforced. backend/internal/users/ shows the canonical split — handler.go handles only HTTP + permission gates and delegates to Svc.*(); service.go holds crypto, transactions, audit; sql.go holds all SQL constants. Same pattern in flows/ (handler 249 LoC, service 399 LoC, sql 377 LoC). The lint:sql-in-sqlfile-only linter fails any non-sql.go file containing raw SQL. The lint:no-db-in-handlers linter forbids pgxpool imports in handler files. Context propagation: 100% — no context.Background() found in handler hot paths. The polymorphicrefs, dbinvariants, transport, and featuretests packages encode architectural invariants as code.

Evidence — frontend (Fair): 14 React Contexts (ScopeContext, AuthContext, NavPrefsContext, DomRegistryContext, SamanthaSdkContext, MasterDebugContext, PageHeaderContext, PageAccessContext, ActiveNavContext, TenantContext, LibraryReleasesContext, ArtefactTypeCatalogueContext, ArtefactPriorityCatalogueContext, DevTabContext) — DomRegistryContext alone defines three context objects. app/lib/apiSite/index.ts is a god-object (1,029 LoC, every route group). app/redesign/ is a parallel shell implementation (11 files: ShellContext, RedesignShell, RedesignTopBar, nav_primary_rail_1, nav_primary_rail_2).

Analysis: The backend pattern is industry-best — lint-enforced, test-mirrored, doc-anchored. The frontend pattern is consistent but mature enough that god-objects and parallel implementations are starting to show — the price of moving fast through a redesign without a written sunset for the previous shell.

2.4 Readability — Good

Evidence: Naming is mostly meaningful — useResourceRank, useKeyboardGrid, useStatusChipReducer, ScopeContext.grants/activeNodeId. Outliers: nav_primary_rail_1.tsx / nav_primary_rail_2.tsx (numeric suffix without an enumeration source); p_ObjectTree.tsx / p_wizard_*.json (the p_ prefix is project-canon for "primitive" but undocumented at the file level). 70 docs index via the c_*.md / c_c_*.md depth-pattern. CLAUDE.md and CONTEXT.md are kept lean, with leaves loaded on demand.

Analysis: The naming convention root-block__Container_Child_leaf for CSS (memory file css_naming_convention.md) is rigorously applied where it appears and tracked by lint. Where it isn't, it's the same files flagged in §2.2 — the hotspots are also the readability weak points.

2.5 Ease of Management — Good

Evidence:

  • Plans: 47 dev/plans/PLA-NNNN.json with structured scope / implementation-plan / status-note fields. PLA-0050 (shipped 2026-05-16) shows the pattern: 10 tasks, areas-impacted matrix, AC verification recorded inline.
  • Tech-debt register: docs/c_tech_debt.md with S1/S2/S3 severity and trigger conditions. Standing rule per CLAUDE.md: every task maintains it.
  • Hooks: 16 Claude hooks in .claude/hooks/ (loop-detector, post-edit-lint, scope-commit-note, hot-paths-session-start, push-backup-gate) + repo-level dev/git-hooks/ (pre-commit, pre-push).
  • Scope tracker: Vector_Scope.md (650 KB, 11 in-flight themes, WIP cap = 5).
  • Migration discipline: 200+ migrations with paired _DOWN.sql files in db/mmff_vector/schema/down/; schema_migrations verified by the <migration> skill.

Analysis: Process scaffolding is unusually heavy for a solo-dev project — it's clearly inherited from a prior prod-ready mode and has not been pruned. The solo-dev memory in feedback_solo_dev_mode.md explicitly freezes the index counters and switches stories to title+AC only, which is the right call until external users return.

2.6 Ease of Future-Proofing — Good

Evidence:

  • Three databases intentionally split: mmff_vector (control), vector_artefacts (cutover substrate hosting artefact_types, artefacts, flows, field_library, timebox_*), mmff_library (read-only library spine). Migration 200 documents the cross-DB writer constraint that motivated the design.
  • Cutover registers: docs/c_c_v1_v2_cutover.md, docs/c_c_v2_workitems_cutover_followups.md, docs/c_c_vector_artefacts_backfill.md — every multi-step migration is tracked in writing.
  • Sole-writer pattern: polymorphicrefs.Write() is the gate for every polymorphic FK insert; DB triggers (migration 013) enforce at the lower layer.
  • Versioned API: app/api/v2/, api-snapshots/v1 and v2, openapi.yaml + openapi-v2.yaml tracked separately.

Analysis: The architecture is explicitly built for evolution: every cutover has a written register, sole-writer rules prevent rogue inserts, and version segregation is real (not aspirational). The risk is timeline — 47 PLA plans show ambition exceeding the solo-dev cap, and the workspace legacy table (parked S3 debt) compounds with each new feature.

2.7 Ease of Maintenance — Good (with one Poor sub-axis)

Evidence — backend (Good): 86 *_test.go files vs 172 non-test = 50% ratio. Dedicated test-only packages (dbinvariants validates DB triggers, featuretests encodes PLA feature contracts as runnable specs). logger package present but uptake incomplete — 37 occurrences of fmt.Println / log.Printf still scattered. Audit logging via backend/internal/audit/audit.go is structured. Error wrapping is inconsistent: ~40% of if err != nil blocks in users/service.go and flows/service.go return bare errors without fmt.Errorf("%w", err) context.

Evidence — frontend (Poor on tests): ~18 test files vs 326 source = ~6% ratio. Tests live under __tests__/ rather than co-located, which hurts the dev feedback loop. Playwright specs in dev/tests/playwright/ exist for drag-and-drop and relations, but coverage of business-logic components is thin.

Analysis: A new contributor on backend code can read tests-as-spec; on frontend they cannot. Combined with the 2,000-LoC pages in §2.2, frontend bug-fix time will scale with file size, not feature complexity.

3. Cross-Cutting Observations

3.1 Duplication & Reusability

5 icon libraries installed simultaneously: feather-icons, react-feather, iconoir, lucide-react, react-icons. Consolidating to one (recommend lucide-react: actively maintained, tree-shakeable) would save bundle size and decision overhead. Parallel app/redesign/ shell duplicates concerns from the main app/ shell — sunset date not documented.

3.2 Documentation Quality

70 docs in docs/ with a depth-prefix system (c_, c_c_, c_c_c_) that scales. Every architectural decision lives somewhere: c_security.md for posture, c_c_backend_validation.md for the golden rule, c_c_db_routing.md for the service-to-pool map, c_tech_debt.md for unresolved trade-offs. No ADR directory — decisions are embedded in docs/superpowers/specs/ design docs + handover. MY_HANDOVER.md tracks parked stories with explicit re-grill cadence.

3.3 Testing & Testability

Backend 50% / frontend 6% is the dominant asymmetry. The featuretests package (f1_workspace_clamp, f3_dto_slot, f4_filters_uuid_shape, f7_priority_substrate, f8_priority_crud_wire) is exemplary — it doubles as a feature-flag specification. Frontend has no equivalent.

3.4 Error Handling & Logging

Inconsistent error-wrapping discipline on the backend (~40% bare returns) and incomplete logger adoption (37 scattered fmt.Println). Audit logs are structured and append-only; application logs are mixed. Frontend uses sonner for toast notifications and console.log for debug — SA001.json flags permission data leaking to console.log in ScopeContext.tsx.

3.5 Security Nuances

The single Critical risk is secrets in plaintext: backend/.env.local and backend/.env.dev contain JWT_ACCESS_SECRET, JWT_REFRESH_SECRET, MASTER_KEY, DB password, and third-party tokens. Gitignored but unrotated. backend/cmd/encsecret/main.go exists but isn't wired to bootstrap. Three High-severity dangerouslySetInnerHTML sites (app/layout.tsx theme name, Header.tsx help body, HelpDocRenderer.tsx) rely on backend sanitisation without client-side guard. CSP is partially set in next.config but not verified strict. CORS in backend/cmd/server/main.go uses env-driven allowed origins (not *). All these items are pre-catalogued in dev/security-audits/SA001.json.

4. Case Studies

4.1 Simple — backend/internal/users/handler.go

249 lines. Reads as a textbook example of HTTP-only handler discipline: every route extracts auth from r.Context() via auth.UserFromCtx(), runs permission checks, decodes JSON, delegates to h.Svc.*(r.Context(), ...), and writes 4xx/5xx via httperr. Zero SQL, zero business logic. Pairs with service.go (transactions + crypto) and sql.go (constants only). Mirrored by service_test.go (13.6 KB) which exercises the service layer directly without HTTP. Verdict: the model the rest of the codebase aspires to.

4.2 Moderate — app/contexts/ScopeContext.tsx

Recently refactored (commit 3946caa): persists active scope to user profile, grouped panel, backend grant check. Proper SSR-guard (typeof window), good use of useCallback / useMemo, single source of truth for grants + activeNodeId. Weakness: console.log calls leak permission data in production (SA001 Medium finding) — needs a NODE_ENV === 'development' guard. Otherwise an exemplary context. The recent commit history shows iterative hardening (CSS tokens, ROW_H constant, hover states) without scope creep — clean PR-equivalent commits.

4.3 Complex — app/(user)/workspace-admin/flow-states/page.tsx

1,628 LoC, 19 inline style={{}} violations (the single highest count in the codebase), embedded business logic (pin/unpin caps as magic numbers MAX_PINNED=50, MAX_CUSTOM_GROUPS=10), hardcoded 18-colour palette array, inline state machine. Diagnosis: this page accreted by feature-flag (flow-states is itself a substrate substrate — see PLA-0024). It needs the RisksPanel-style decomposition into FlowStatesPanel + FlowStatePalette + a CSS class file. The inline-style violations are not a debt entry — they're invisible to lint because no ratchet exists for style={{ in this directory. Adding a lint with file-level exempt registry would let the file ratchet down without a big-bang refactor.

5. Recommendations

5.1 Critical (close before further feature work)

  1. Close SA001 High findings. Encrypt .env.local / .env.dev via existing backend/cmd/encsecret or migrate to a secrets manager. Add DOMPurify or equivalent to the three dangerouslySetInnerHTML sites. Verify no prod secrets in git history: git log -p -- '*env.production*'.
  2. Gate /api/dev/* on auth + permission, not NODE_ENV. Currently only the env flag protects them.
  3. Strip console.log of permission data in ScopeContext.tsx and similar — wrap in NODE_ENV === 'development' guard or remove.

5.2 Structural (refactor pressure points)

  1. Decompose the four 1,400+ LoC pages (preferences/navigation, flow-states, theme, ResourceTree). Use the existing Panel + hook pattern; carry rules into co-located __tests__.
  2. Split app/lib/apiSite/index.ts into per-domain files (apiSite/auth.ts, apiSite/workspaces.ts, etc.) — the 1,029-LoC registry blocks tree-shaking and forces every API change through one file.
  3. Document the app/redesign/ sunset — write a tech-debt entry with a trigger condition (e.g. "when nav-prefs lands") or commit to it as the new shell and start removing the old.
  4. Add ratchet lint for inline style={{ using the existing exemption-ratchet harness (lint:exemption-ratchet already exists). Seed exempt-list with the 92 current violations; new ones fail CI.

5.3 Housekeeping

  1. Consolidate icon libraries to one (lucide-react). Remove the other four from package.json after a grep-sweep.
  2. Adopt logger uniformly on the backend. Add a lint forbidding fmt.Println / log.Printf in backend/internal/** (37 current occurrences become the seed list).
  3. Standardise error wrapping — fmt.Errorf("users: %w", err) on every non-sentinel return.
  4. Co-locate frontend tests next to source rather than under __tests__/ — speeds the dev feedback loop.

5.4 Process

  1. Enable Dependabot (or weekly npm audit + govulncheck) — no current automated dep scanning.
  2. Audit the parked S2 / S3 debt in MY_HANDOVER.md against current code before next cutover — "re-grill against live code before action" is the documented rule.
  3. Verify .github/workflows/ coverage — only api-contracts.yml and tests.yml visible; confirm both frontend + backend tests run on every push.

6. Conclusion

Vector is a healthy, opinionated monolith. The backend is the standout — clean layering, lint-enforced rules, dedicated invariant test packages, paired migrations, and ~zero detected SQL-injection or context-propagation defects. The frontend is consistent and well-typed but shows the wear of rapid iteration in three predictable shapes: large admin pages, a god-object API registry, and a parallel redesign shell. Security posture is well-documented; the gap is in operational hygiene (secrets, XSS guards, dev-endpoint auth) rather than in design — and the gap is already catalogued in SA001.json.

Estimated remediation time: Critical items (SA001 High×3 + dev-endpoint gating + console.log strip) ≈ 1-2 days. Structural items (4 page decompositions, apiSite split, redesign sunset, ratchet lint) ≈ 2-3 weeks. Housekeeping ≈ 2-3 days.

Confidence: Medium-High. High on static signals (file sizes, lint rules, dependency surface, schema discipline, layering). Medium on dynamic correctness (concurrency, race conditions, runtime CSP/cookie verification) — those need runtime instrumentation that was out of scope.

C. Suggested metrics to track

  • Cyclomatic-complexity hotspots — the four 1,400+ LoC pages and ResourceTree.tsx; baseline + monthly trend.
  • Inline-style count — current baseline 92 across app/. Ratchet via lint:exemption-ratchet; trend should be monotonically decreasing.
  • Frontend test-to-source ratio — currently ~6%. Target ≥20% for app/components/ primitives within 6 months.
  • Backend error-wrap ratio — fmt.Errorf("%w", err) as % of if err != nil. Currently ~60%; target ≥95%.
  • fmt.Println / log.Printf count in backend/internal/ — currently 37; target 0 (use logger).
  • Tech-debt S1 count — track count over time in docs/c_tech_debt.md; should trend down or stay flat.
  • PLA in-flight vs WIP cap — currently 11 themes in Vector_Scope.md, cap is 5. Cap-pressure metric.
